quote: I've just seen a lot of advertisement about it, and I haven't done it because I figured it'd be expensive to do.
stewart_read: Robert hands the rep his barrier in the first substantive line of the call. The correct move per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring is capture + flip: anchor to his current bill pain and invert the cost assumption against it. The rep instead responds with a generic 'no money out of pocket / bill swap' claim — a feature statement, not an emotional anchor. The bill hasn't even been captured yet, so the flip has no number to land on.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep may have been deliberately deferring the flip until the bill was captured, intending to return to it with a real number. Evidence against: the rep never returned to it even after capturing the $100–$200 range at 02:35.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the two-touch pattern: cost anxiety raised at 00:44, bill captured at 02:35, flip never executed. The coaching move is to practice the capture-then-flip sequence: once the bill number is in hand, turn it back on the utility. Suggested intent (not verbatim): 'So you're already paying up to $200 a month to PSO — that's the expense. Solar replaces that expense, it doesn't add to it.' The rep needs to feel the difference between a feature claim and an inversion.

quote: I think I have, like, a six zero four. My wife has a higher one. She's close to 700, and she's on she's on the mortgage too.
stewart_read: Robert confirms his wife is on the title and has the qualifying credit score — she is both the financial qualifier and a co-decision-maker. The rep correctly notes this ('Okay. Awesome. Yeah. So that'll be perfect there.') but does not explicitly confirm the wife will be present for the appointment. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the co-title-holder must be on the appointment. The rep books the sit at 04:38 without revisiting this.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have assumed that booking with Robert implicitly includes the wife since she's on the title. Evidence against: the button-up at 04:51 addresses only Robert ('I'll send you a text as well') with no explicit confirmation that the wife will be present.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, pull up 04:38–04:51 and ask: 'Where did you confirm the wife would be on the call?' The rep needs to add one line at booking: 'And we'll make sure [wife's name] is on that call with you, right?' This is a paycheck-protection move — if the wife isn't present and she's the qualifier, the sit may not hold.

stewart_read: The bill range is captured but the rep's response — 'Dang. Yeah. That's quite the range there. It's crazy.' — is a social acknowledgment, not an anchor. Robert immediately contextualizes the range (new house, gas appliances, uncertainty about summer AC costs), which is actually a forward-looking bill anxiety signal. The rep responds with 'Okay. Sweet. Well, yeah, congrats on the new home.' — rapport, not leverage. The flip opportunity is present and unused.
coaching_implication: The rep has the number and the context — Robert is worried about what summer bills will look like. That's the anchor: 'So you don't know yet how high it'll get in summer — that's exactly why locking in solar now makes sense before you find out.' The manager should walk the rep through how the bill context Robert volunteered (new house, gas appliances, AC uncertainty) was actually a richer setup for the flip than a simple high bill would have been.

quote: the last thing to do now is to just find a good time for one of our specialists to stop by, just kinda review the information with you
stewart_read: The rep uses 'stop by' language, which frames the appointment as an in-person visit. Per button_up.phone_appointment_specified, the appointment is a phone call — this is a hard-required schema element. Robert may now be expecting someone to come to his home. The button-up at 04:48 ('I'll send you a text as well with just the day and time') does not correct this framing.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have been using 'stop by' loosely as a colloquial expression for 'drop in on a call.' Evidence against: there is no subsequent clarification that the appointment is by phone, and the button-up does not include the 'no driving, at your computer' anchor required by button_up.no_driving_at_computer.
coaching_implication: This is a no-show risk. If Robert expects an in-person visit and a phone call comes instead, the sit is at risk. The closer should confirm the format before the appointment. In the 1-on-1, drill the rep on the phone-appointment language: 'I've got one of our specialists scheduled to call you at 3:30 — you'll want to be somewhere you can pull up the design on your computer.' That line also covers button_up.no_driving_at_computer, which was also skipped.

quote: the thing about it is me and my wife both work night shift. I work the second half of the week. She works the first half of the week. But we wake up at two every day and, but we have to get kids from school. But then we leave for work at five. So it would have to be between, like, 03:30 and five. It doesn't matter what day if they're able to do that.
stewart_read: Robert is actively problem-solving to make the appointment happen — he maps out his entire household schedule unprompted to find a window. This is the clearest buy-in signal in the call. The rep matched it correctly by pulling up the calendar and booking the slot. The missed opportunity is not in the booking itself but in not anchoring the wife's presence here: Robert just told the rep exactly when both he and his wife are home together (between 2 PM and 5 PM). That's the natural moment to confirm she'll be on the call.
coaching_implication: Use this moment in the 1-on-1 as a positive anchor — the rep read the signal and moved. Then extend the coaching: 'You had the perfect setup right here to confirm the wife. He just told you they're both home at the same time. What would you have said?' Build the habit of treating scheduling-detail disclosures as spouse-confirmation opportunities.

quote: the requirements to qualify are to have a form of taxable income and a credit score of six fifty or higher.
stewart_read: The rep states the credit threshold as 650. The schema records 670 as the threshold, with a note that SESSION18_fcbf33c1 also stated 650 — flagged as a drift observation. This call adds a second instance of the 650 figure. Either the floor is operating on a different number than the schema reflects, or there is a consistent rep-level drift. Separately, Robert discloses a 604 score and the rep qualifies him through his wife's score — which is the correct move, but the threshold discrepancy should be reconciled.
alternative_hypothesis: The active threshold may genuinely be 650, not 670, and the schema is out of date. This is a policy question for Kenny, not a rep error.
coaching_implication: Do not coach the rep on this until the threshold is confirmed with Kenny. Flag it for the manager to resolve: two reps have now stated 650 on recorded calls. If 670 is the correct number, there is a floor-wide calibration gap. If 650 is current policy, the schema needs updating. Either way, the manager needs the answer before coaching the rep.

quote: That's been my biggest concern is I always heard how expensive it was. To for the panels and the batteries and all the things.
stewart_read: Robert volunteers his core barrier a second time, after the appointment is already booked. This is the second touch of the cost-anxiety pattern (first touch: 00:44). The rep's response — 'I think it's gonna be cool to kinda just see how the programs work and, yeah, just see what your options are' — is a soft, non-committal close. The flip was available: Robert just named the exact pain point ('panels and batteries and all the things') that the inversion targets. Suggested intent (not verbatim): 'That's exactly why people are surprised — what's expensive is what you're already paying PSO every month. Solar replaces that cost, it doesn't add to it.' The rep instead deferred to the closer.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep may have deliberately left the cost resolution to the closer, reasoning that the appointment is already booked and reopening the cost conversation risks creating new objections. This is a defensible read — the booking is secured. Evidence against: the rep's language ('it's gonna be cool to see') is not a deliberate deferral; it's a soft close that leaves the anxiety live without framing it as intentional setup for the closer.
coaching_implication: This is the flip-opportunity coaching moment for this call. The manager should play 00:44 and 05:19 back-to-back and ask the rep: 'He told you the same thing twice. What did you do with it?' Then introduce the inversion framework: the cost concern is the lever, not the obstacle. The rep doesn't need to resolve it fully — but they need to reframe it so Robert walks into the sit curious, not anxious.

quote: I'll send you a text as well with just the day and time. So if someone comes up and you need to get that approved, just let me know.
stewart_read: The rep's button-up is thin: no 'is that fair' verbal handshake (button_up.is_that_fair_handshake), no 'no driving, at your computer' anchor (button_up.no_driving_at_computer), no one-hour block stated (button_up.one_hour_block_stated), and the text promise is generic ('just the day and time') rather than personalized per text_after_call.required_elements. The phrase 'if someone comes up and you need to get that approved' is the only acknowledgment of the wife's role — and it frames her as an approver of a done deal rather than a required participant.
coaching_implication: The button-up is missing three of six required elements. In the 1-on-1, walk through the button_up checklist against this call's close. The rep should be able to identify what's missing. Priority fix: the 'is that fair' handshake and the computer/no-driving anchor — both are directly tied to show rate. Secondary fix: the text should reference something Robert said (e.g., his concern about cost, his new home) to personalize it per text_after_call requirements.
